examples.title: Simple Virtual Machine Monitor Pseudocode
examples.description: Basic pseudocode demonstrating VMM functions of resource allocation and VM management
examples.code:
VMM-CREATE-VM(config)
BEGIN
  CREATE newVM
  newVM.vcpu = config.cpuCount
  newVM.memory = config.memorySize
  newVM.disk = config.diskSize
  newVM.state = STOPPED
  RETURN newVM
END

VMM-START-VM(vm)
BEGIN
  IF vm.state = STOPPED THEN
    ALLOCATE-HARDWARE-RESOURCES(vm)
    LOAD-GUEST-OS(vm)
    vm.state = RUNNING
    SCHEDULE-VM(vm)
  ENDIF
END

VMM-SCHEDULE-VM(vm)
BEGIN
  WHILE vm.state = RUNNING
    DISPATCH-VM(vm)
    SAVE-VM-CONTEXT(vm)
    RUNNING-VMs = GET-RUNNING-VMS()
    NEXT-VM = SELECT-SCHEDULING-ALGORITHM(RUNNING-VMs)
  ENDWHILE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates basic VMM operations: creating VMs with specified resources, starting VMs by loading guest OS, and CPU scheduling among multiple running VMs.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for scheduling where n is number of VMs

examples.title: VM Resource Allocation Simulation in Python
examples.description: Python code simulating how hypervisor allocates CPU and memory resources to multiple VMs
examples.code:
class VirtualMachine:
    def __init__(self, vm_id, cpu_cores, memory_mb):
        self.vm_id = vm_id
        self.cpu_cores = cpu_cores
        self.memory_mb = memory_mb
        self.state = 'STOPPED'

class Hypervisor:
    def __init__(self, total_cpu_cores, total_memory_mb):
        self.total_cpu_cores = total_cpu_cores
        self.total_memory_mb = total_memory_mb
        self.available_cpu = total_cpu_cores
        self.available_memory = total_memory_mb
        self.vms = []

    def create_vm(self, vm_id, cpu_cores, memory_mb):
        if (cpu_cores <= self.available_cpu and 
            memory_mb <= self.available_memory):
            vm = VirtualMachine(vm_id, cpu_cores, memory_mb)
            self.vms.append(vm)
            self.available_cpu -= cpu_cores
            self.available_memory -= memory_mb
            return f"VM {vm_id} created successfully"
        return f"Insufficient resources for VM {vm_id}"

    def start_vm(self, vm_id):
        for vm in self.vms:
            if vm.vm_id == vm_id:
                vm.state = 'RUNNING'
                return f"VM {vm_id} started"
        return f"VM {vm_id} not found"

# Example Usage
hv = Hypervisor(total_cpu_cores=16, total_memory_mb=65536)
print(hv.create_vm("VM1", 4, 8192))
print(hv.create_vm("VM2", 4, 8192))
print(hv.start_vm("VM1"))
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates a basic hypervisor managing CPU and memory resources. It demonstrates resource allocation, tracking available resources, and VM state management.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for VM lookup and resource checks
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing n VM objects

examples.title: VM Snapshot Management Pseudocode
examples.description: Pseudocode for creating and restoring VM snapshots
examples.code:
SNAPSHOT-CREATE(vm, snapshotName)
BEGIN
  snapshot = ALLOCATE-SNAPSHOT-STRUCT()
  snapshot.name = snapshotName
  snapshot.timestamp = CURRENT-TIME()
  snapshot.memoryState = CAPTURE-VM-MEMORY(vm)
  snapshot.diskState = COPY-VM-DISK(vm.disk)
  snapshot.cpuState = SAVE-VM-REGISTERS(vm)
  vm.snapshots.add(snapshot)
  RETURN snapshot
END

SNAPSHOT-RESTORE(vm, snapshotName)
BEGIN
  snapshot = FIND-SNAPSHOT(vm.snapshots, snapshotName)
  IF snapshot = NULL THEN
    RETURN "Snapshot not found"
  ENDIF
  
  PAUSE-VM(vm)
  RESTORE-VM-REGISTERS(vm, snapshot.cpuState)
  RESTORE-VM-MEMORY(vm, snapshot.memoryState)
  COPY-DISK-IMAGE(vm.disk, snapshot.diskState)
  vm.state = snapshot.savedState
  RETURN "Snapshot restored successfully"
END

SNAPSHOT-LIST(vm)
BEGIN
  FOR EACH snap IN vm.snapshots
    PRINT snap.name, snap.timestamp
  ENDFOR
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows VM snapshot operations: creating snapshots by capturing memory, disk, and CPU state; restoring snapshots to return VM to previous state; and listing available snapshots.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for create/restore (excluding I/O), O(n) for list with n snapshots
examples.spaceComplexity: O(vm.memory + vm.disk) for storing snapshot data
